CHAPTER 4 — LESSON PLANNING AND ACTIVITY DESIGN

Deshawn teaches high school chemistry. He asks Claude for a lesson plan on chemical reactions and gets back three paragr

Chapter 4 — Lesson Planning and Activity — He posts it to his shared drive and starts printing the worksheet.
NARRATION

Deshawn teaches high school chemistry. He asks Claude for a lesson plan on chemical reactions and gets back three paragraphs introducing the topic, a teacher-led demonstration, a worksheet with ten definitional questions, and a homework problem set. The plan is complete. It has a learning objective, materials list, timing, and closure. It looks like every other lesson plan he has seen. He posts it to his shared drive and starts printing the worksheet.

// SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.

·Use Claude to generate lesson-plan drafts anchored in clear learning outcomes.
Audit a Claude-generated plan for cognitive laborwhether students are doing the thinking.
·Redesign teacher-centered sequences into active learning alternatives.
·Use Claude to produce targeted lesson components rather than complete plans.
·Apply a five-question review gate before using any Claude-generated plan.
NARRATION

By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Use Claude to generate lesson-plan drafts anchored in clear learning outcomes. Audit a Claude-generated plan for cognitive labor , whether students are doing the thinking. Redesign teacher-centered sequences into active learning alternatives. Use Claude to produce targeted lesson components rather than complete plans. Apply a five-question review gate before using any Claude-generated plan.

// SLIDE 03 — CORE CONCEPT — BACKWARD DESIGN

CORE CONCEPT — BACKWARD DESIGN AND COGNITIVE LABOR

Two principles govern good lesson planning. The first is backward design. The second is cognitive labor.

Core Concept — Backward Design and Cogni — Backward design, developed by Wiggins and McTighe in Understanding by Design, holds that planning should begin with the
NARRATION

Two principles govern good lesson planning. The first is backward design. The second is cognitive labor. Backward design, developed by Wiggins and McTighe in Understanding by Design, holds that planning should begin with the evidence of learning , what would count as acceptable proof that students achieved the outcome , and work backward to instruction. Most lesson plans work forward: topic, activities, then assessment. Backward design reverses this.

// SLIDE 04 — A WORKED TEACHING WALKTHROUGH

A WORKED TEACHING WALKTHROUGH.

·"Draft three discussion questions for this activity. Each question should be unanswerable by recalling a definition."
·"Generate four plausible student thesis statements ranging from vague to specific, using this topic. I will use these as
"Draft an exit ticket with two questionsone that assesses whether students achieved the outcome, and one that asks them to name one thing they are still unsure
·"Suggest two formative-check moments I can embed in this sequence without adding more than five minutes total."
NARRATION

You teach a college writing course. Your next class session is ninety minutes. Students have submitted a first draft of an argumentative essay. Your learning outcome: students will revise a weak thesis statement using specific evidence from their own drafts, not general writing advice. Most students believe their theses are already specific. Your experience tells you they confuse specificity with confidence of tone , a thesis can sound certain while saying very little.

// SLIDE 05 — THE HUMAN GATE — WHAT STAYS WI

THE HUMAN GATE — WHAT STAYS WITH YOU

Pacing and classroom reality.→Content accuracy.→Sequence logic for your specific unit.→Who needs what.→The relationship dimension.
NARRATION

Lesson planning with Claude is a design iteration process, not a production process. The following decisions belong to you: Pacing and classroom reality. Claude does not know that your Tuesday section always arrives ten minutes distracted after gym class, that your building runs fire drills in November without notice, or that three students in your third period have modified schedules. Timing adjustments are yours.

// SLIDE 06 — COMMON MISTAKES

COMMON MISTAKES

Mistake 1: Accepting the complete plan.→Mistake 2: Requesting a lesson plan without stating the outcome first.→Mistake 3: Keeping the teacher at the center of every activity.→Mistake 4: Using Claude's examples without checking them.→Mistake 5: Ignoring the formative feedback loop.
NARRATION

Mistake 1: Accepting the complete plan. Complete plans are complete. That is their surface quality. Completeness is not alignment. Audit the plan; do not accept its completeness as evidence of quality. Mistake 2: Requesting a lesson plan without stating the outcome first. If you do not anchor the prompt in a specific outcome, Claude will generate content-coverage activities. Always start with what students should be able to do.

// SLIDE 07 — BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 5

BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 5.

A well-designed lesson generates student work. That work requires feedback. Chapter 5 addresses the moment where AI assi

NARRATION

A well-designed lesson generates student work. That work requires feedback. Chapter 5 addresses the moment where AI assistance is most ethically loaded: when Claude is helping you respond to what a specific student has actually done. Feedback is not lesson planning. It is judgment about a person's learning. The human gate there is sharper, and the risks of overhelping are more direct.
